metadata.title: Operation Chokepoint 2.0, regulation-by-enforcement asymmetry, and the crypto-bank failure nodes (FDIC/OCC/Fed/Treasury pause letters; FTX/Silvergate/Signature/SVB/Flagstar; SEC Corp Fin)
metadata.frame: The 2022-2024 crypto-bank squeeze is a clean case of selective state pressure through the banking perimeter rather than through law. Three threads: (1) 'Operation Chokepoint 2.0' - coordinated FDIC/OCC/Fed/Treasury supervisory pressure on banks to 'pause' crypto activity, evidenced by the FDIC 'pause letters' FOIA-released in 2025; (2) regulation-by-enforcement ASYMMETRY - crypto-native issuers (Ripple/XRP) sued while incumbent banks were permitted to build tokenized-deposit/coin products (JPM Coin), an incumbent-favoring pattern; (3) the bank failures (Silvergate, Signature, SVB) and the resolution choices (Flagstar/NYCB taking Signature's non-crypto deposits) that reshaped who banks crypto.
metadata.WARNING: Grade discipline. 'Chokepoint 2.0' is a CONTESTED political framing; the underlying FDIC pause letters and the Custodia master-account denial are DOCUMENTED facts. Coordinated INTENT to debank an industry is alleged and partially evidenced (letters, testimony), not adjudicated - graded contested. The 'JPM Coin timed just after Ripple' claim is CHRONOLOGICALLY INVERTED and corrected below; the structural incumbent-favoritism it points at is real. No claim that any specific official acted with corrupt intent - this maps documented pressure and asymmetry, not verdicts. Overlay edges (regulatory_legal / relationship) are EXCLUDED from the SCC/Z3/TLA+ proofs.
metadata.method: FDIC FOIA productions (the 'pause letters', released 2025 in Coinbase v. FDIC litigation); House Financial Services & Senate Banking testimony; Custodia Bank v. Federal Reserve docket; SEC v. Ripple (S.D.N.Y. 2020-2024) and the Torres ruling; SEC litigation releases; Fed/OCC/FDIC joint statements on crypto (Jan 2023); bank-failure post-mortems (Fed 'Barr report' on SVB; FDIC on Signature). Each cell graded fact|contested|weak|unsupported.

operation_chokepoint_2.summary: A pattern of non-public supervisory pressure (2022-2024) discouraging banks from serving crypto clients, named by critics after the Obama-era 'Operation Choke Point' (which pressured banks away from payday/firearms/other 'high-risk' merchants).

documented_facts.detail: In 2025, under FOIA in Coinbase's litigation, the FDIC released ~24 letters it had sent to banks asking them to PAUSE or refrain from expanding crypto-related activities pending further guidance. The letters are real; they show the FDIC actively discouraging crypto banking.

The Fed, FDIC, and OCC issued joint statements warning of crypto 'safety and soundness' risks and (Fed policy statement) effectively discouraging banks from holding crypto or issuing dollar tokens on public/permissionless chains.

The Fed denied Custodia (a Wyoming SPDI, crypto-focused) a master account and membership (2023); courts upheld the denial. A concrete instance of the Fed gatekeeping a crypto bank out of the payment system.

In 2025 the new FDIC (acting chair Travis Hill), OCC (Gould), and Fed leadership rescinded the restrictive crypto guidance and the pause posture - an implicit confirmation that a discouraging posture had existed.

operation_chokepoint_2.contested: Whether this constituted a COORDINATED, intentional campaign to debank a lawful industry (the 'Chokepoint 2.0' thesis) - alleged by the industry and some legislators (the documents and testimony support a discouraging posture; a single orchestrated directive is not proven). Graded CONTESTED.

regulation_by_enforcement_asymmetry.summary: Crypto-native dollar/asset issuers faced enforcement while incumbent banks were permitted to build tokenized-deposit and coin products - an incumbent-favoring asymmetry.

ripple_case.detail: SEC v. Ripple Labs filed Dec 22, 2020, alleging XRP was an unregistered security. Judge Torres (July 2023) ruled programmatic/exchange XRP sales were NOT securities offerings, but institutional sales WERE - a split that itself showed the classification incoherence the industry decried as 'regulation by enforcement.'

jpm_coin_chronology_correction.claim_as_posed: 'JPM planned JPM Coin just after Ripple got targeted by the SEC.'
jpm_coin_chronology_correction.correction: CHRONOLOGY INVERTED. JPM Coin was announced Feb 2019 - ~22 months BEFORE the SEC sued Ripple (Dec 2020). So JPM Coin did not follow the Ripple action; it preceded it.
jpm_coin_chronology_correction.what_survives: The STRUCTURAL point is still valid: a regulated bank (JPMorgan) was permitted to launch and expand a dollar token (JPM Coin, then the JPMD deposit token on Base, 2025) with no SEC enforcement, while crypto-native issuers faced suits. The asymmetry is real even though the specific temporal sequencing in the claim is reversed.
jpm_coin_chronology_correction.grade: the timing claim is FALSE as stated (corrected); the incumbent-favoritism pattern is fact/contested

regulation_by_enforcement_asymmetry.pattern: Incumbent banks (JPMorgan, later Citi/BofA tokenized-deposit pilots) operated dollar tokens under banking law while Coinbase, Ripple, Kraken, Binance faced SEC suits. Whether by design or by the accident of which legal regime applies, the effect favored incumbents - the same dynamic the GENIUS Act later codified (bank tokenized deposits preserved; nonbank stablecoins constrained).
regulation_by_enforcement_asymmetry.grade: fact (the disparate treatment) + interpretation labeled (whether intentional)

sec_corp_fin.summary: The SEC's Division of Corporation Finance (the disclosure-review and no-action/guidance arm) is where the quiet, consequential calls on what must be disclosed - and which offerings get a pass - are made.

points.detail: Corp Fin's comment letters and informal guidance shape which crypto and SPAC offerings proceed; critics across regimes allege inconsistency favoring connected issuers. Specific 'corruption' allegations are NOT established in the public record and are NOT asserted here.
points.grade: weak/unsupported (specific corruption); fact (the division's gatekeeping role)

points.detail: Staff Accounting Bulletin 121 (2022, Corp Fin/OCA) forced firms custodying crypto to put it on-balance-sheet - a major disincentive for banks to custody crypto; it was rescinded (SAB 122) in 2025. A documented, reversible staff-level lever with large industry effect.

sec_corp_fin.honest_note: The user's 'corruption around Corp Fin in former regimes' is directionally about selective gatekeeping; the DOCUMENTED mechanism is staff guidance (SAB 121, no-action posture) used as policy. Specific corrupt-intent allegations against named Corp Fin officials are not substantiated in the public record - left UNSUPPORTED rather than fabricated.

bank_failure_nodes.role: The original crypto bank (SEN real-time settlement network). Heavily exposed to FTX/Alameda and crypto deposits; suffered a deposit run after FTX's Nov-2022 collapse and self-liquidated Mar 2023. The first domino.
bank_failure_nodes.ftx_link: FTX/Alameda were major Silvergate clients; the SEN network moved fiat between crypto firms.

bank_failure_nodes.role: NY crypto-friendly bank (Signet network). Seized by NYDFS/FDIC Mar 12, 2023 - the systemic-risk-exception weekend. Regulators were later accused of using the failure to send an anti-crypto signal (board member Barney Frank's claim); the FDIC disputed crypto was the reason (cited liquidity/management).
bank_failure_nodes.ftx_link: Held crypto-industry deposits; Signet was a crypto fiat rail.
bank_failure_nodes.resolution: Flagstar Bank (a NYCB subsidiary) acquired most Signature deposits and branches from the FDIC - but EXPLICITLY EXCLUDED the ~$4B of crypto-related deposits, which the FDIC retained and wound down. The crypto deposits were deliberately stranded.

bank_failure_nodes.role: Not a crypto bank per se, but the venture/tech-deposit bank whose Mar 2023 failure (HTM-loss + uninsured-run, the S.2155-threshold cohort) triggered the USDC depeg ($3.3B of Circle's reserves were at SVB; USDC fell to ~$0.87 before the backstop). The link between the bank-failure node and the stablecoin node.
bank_failure_nodes.ftx_link: Indirect: SVB's failure is what depegged USDC, connecting the bank-failure and stablecoin-failure maps.

bank_failure_nodes.bank: Flagstar Bank / New York Community Bancorp (NYCB)
bank_failure_nodes.role: Acquired Signature's non-crypto deposits (Mar 2023). NYCB then suffered its OWN crisis in 2024 - a CRE-loss and reserve shock (Q4-2023 surprise loss, dividend cut, rating downgrade, $1B+ rescue capital injection led by Steven Mnuchin's Liberty Strategic Capital). So the bank that absorbed Signature became a CRE-stress node a year later - and Mnuchin (ex-Treasury, Gulf-backed PE) is the rescuer, tying back to the revolving-door/Gulf-capital threads.
bank_failure_nodes.ftx_link: None direct; the chain is Signature -> Flagstar/NYCB -> 2024 CRE crisis -> Mnuchin rescue.

bank_failure_nodes.role: A tiny (~$100M) rural Washington bank that Alameda Research (FTX) invested ~$11.5M into in 2022 - more than the bank's entire prior net worth - and rebranded 'Moonstone.' A documented FTX attempt to acquire a banking foothold; it wound down after FTX collapsed.
bank_failure_nodes.ftx_link: DIRECT - Alameda/FTX equity investment; one of the clearest FTX-into-banking moves.

ftx_political_regulatory_nodes.summary: FTX/SBF cultivated regulators and Congress while seeking a favorable framework - a documented access pattern (not, by itself, proof the access changed outcomes).

points.detail: Sam Bankman-Fried was among the largest 2022-cycle Democratic donors (~$40M openly) and Ryan Salame funded Republicans (~$24M) - a deliberate both-sides influence spend; later central to SBF's campaign-finance charges (dropped from trial but admitted conduct).

SBF met repeatedly with CFTC chair Rostin Behnam while FTX lobbied for the DCCPA bill that would have given the CFTC (seen as lighter-touch) crypto-spot authority - i.e., FTX lobbying to choose its own regulator.
The Digital Commodities Consumer Protection Act - FTX-favored, would have routed crypto to the CFTC; collapsed with FTX. A bill whose terms were shaped by the firm it would have regulated.

edges.note: OVERLAY: crypto-bank gatekept out of the payment system

edges.raw_instrument: SEC v. Ripple (Dec 2020); Torres split ruling (2023)
bank dollar token (2019) -> JPMD deposit token on Base (2025), no SEC enforcement
FTX/Alameda major client; SEN fiat rail
Alameda ~$11.5M equity into a tiny WA bank (2022)
acquired Signature deposits/branches ex-crypto (FDIC, Mar 2023); ~$4B crypto deposits stranded
edges.note: OVERLAY: resolution deliberately excluded crypto deposits

edges.raw_instrument: Mnuchin-led ~$1B+ rescue of NYCB/Flagstar (2024 CRE crisis)
edges.note: OVERLAY: ex-Treasury/Gulf-PE rescuer ties to revolving-door thread

edges.raw_instrument: $3.3B USDC reserves at SVB -> Mar 2023 depeg to ~$0.87
edges.note: OVERLAY: bank-failure node -> stablecoin-failure node link

limits: This maps DOCUMENTED pressure (FDIC pause letters, Custodia denial, joint statements, SAB 121) and DOCUMENTED asymmetry (Ripple suit vs JPM Coin permitted), and corrects the inverted JPM-Coin/Ripple chronology. It does NOT adjudicate coordinated intent ('Chokepoint 2.0' as an orchestrated campaign = contested) and does NOT assert corruption by any named official (left unsupported rather than fabricated). The bank-failure linkages (Silvergate/Signature/SVB/Flagstar/Farmington) are fact; their causal weighting (how much crypto-debanking vs. ordinary liquidity/CRE risk drove each failure) is partly contested and cross-referenced to macro-bank-htm-marks / macro-fdic.
